title_en: Nikah and Financial Transactions: Marriage and Commerce
content_en:
The chapter on nikah (marriage) in Zad al-Mustaqni' begins with the legal standing of marriage: it is obligatory for one who fears falling into fornication and is able to fulfill marital duties; recommended for one who has desire and ability; and permissible for one who has no desire but no fear of harm. This nuanced approach reflects the Hanbali attention to individual circumstances in determining legal rulings.

The pillars of a valid marriage contract in Hanbali fiqh are three: the offer (ijab) from the guardian (wali) or his representative, the acceptance (qabul) from the groom or his representative, and the presence of two witnesses. The Hanbali school, like the Maliki and Shafi'i schools, requires the wali for the validity of the marriage contract. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'There is no marriage without a guardian' (Ahmad, Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidhi — hasan sahih).

Mahrs (dowers) are obligatory upon the husband. No minimum amount is stipulated, though it should be something of value. If no mahr is specified, the wife is entitled to the mahr al-mithl (the dower customary for women of her family and social standing). If the husband divorces before consummation and without specifying a mahr, she receives a mut'ah (gift of consolation) based on his means.

Divorce (talaq) is permissible but strongly discouraged without legitimate reason. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'Of all permissible things, divorce is the most hated by Allah' (Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah). In the Hanbali school, divorce pronounced three times in one sitting counts as three divorces — a position that differs from some contemporary scholarly views. An irrevocably divorced woman (thrice-divorced) may not remarry her former husband until she has genuinely married another man who then dies or divorces her.

In the realm of financial transactions (mu'amalat), Zad al-Mustaqni' covers sale (bay'), lease (ijarah), agency (wakalah), partnership (sharikah), gifts (hibah), and inheritance (mawarith). The foundational principle is that transactions are permissible unless evidence prohibits them. All forms of riba (interest/usury) are strictly prohibited based on the Quranic prohibition (2:275–279) and numerous ahadith.

A valid sale requires: a seller and buyer with legal capacity, a specific commodity, a specific price, and a valid offer and acceptance. Sales involving gharar (excessive uncertainty) are prohibited. This includes selling fish in water, unborn animals, or crops before they show signs of maturity. The Prophet (peace be upon him) forbade such transactions to protect both parties from financial harm.

Partnership (sharikah) in its various forms is permitted. The most common in classical fiqh are: sharikah al-'inan (capital partnership where each partner contributes capital and profits are shared proportionally), sharikah al-abdan (labor partnership among craftsmen), and sharikah al-wujuh (credit partnership). All forms require clear agreement on profit shares and bear loss according to capital contribution.

Zad al-Mustaqni' concludes its coverage of mu'amalat by emphasizing that the Muslim's commercial dealings must embody truthfulness, transparency, and fairness — qualities the Prophet (peace be upon him) exemplified as a merchant in his youth and praised in others throughout his life.
title_id: Nikah dan Muamalat: Pernikahan dan Perdagangan
content_id:
Bab nikah dalam Zad al-Mustaqni' dimulai dengan kedudukan hukum pernikahan: wajib bagi orang yang khawatir terjerumus ke dalam zina dan mampu memenuhi kewajiban pernikahan; dianjurkan bagi orang yang memiliki hasrat dan kemampuan; dan diperbolehkan bagi orang yang tidak memiliki hasrat tetapi tidak khawatir akan bahaya. Pendekatan bernuansa ini mencerminkan perhatian Hanbali terhadap keadaan-keadaan individu dalam menentukan hukum-hukum fikih.

Rukun-rukun akad nikah yang sah dalam fikih Hanbali ada tiga: ijab dari wali atau wakilnya, qabul dari mempelai laki-laki atau wakilnya, dan kehadiran dua orang saksi. Madzhab Hanbali, seperti madzhab Maliki dan Syafi'i, mensyaratkan adanya wali untuk keabsahan akad nikah. Nabi ﷺ bersabda: 'Tidak ada nikah kecuali dengan wali' (Ahmad, Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidzi — hasan shahih).

Mahar wajib atas suami. Tidak ada jumlah minimum yang ditentukan, meskipun harus berupa sesuatu yang bernilai. Jika mahar tidak ditentukan, istri berhak atas mahr al-mitsl (mahar yang lazim bagi wanita dari keluarga dan tingkat sosialnya). Jika suami menceraikan sebelum konsumasi dan tanpa menentukan mahar, istri mendapatkan mut'ah (hadiah penghiburan) berdasarkan kemampuannya.

Talak (perceraian) diperbolehkan tetapi sangat tidak dianjurkan tanpa alasan yang sah. Nabi ﷺ bersabda: 'Di antara perkara-perkara yang halal, talak adalah yang paling dibenci Allah' (Abu Dawud, Ibnu Majah). Dalam madzhab Hanbali, talak yang diucapkan tiga kali dalam satu majelis dihitung sebagai tiga talak — sebuah posisi yang berbeda dari beberapa pandangan ilmiah kontemporer. Wanita yang ditalak ba'in kubra (tiga kali talak) tidak boleh menikah kembali dengan mantan suaminya hingga ia benar-benar menikah dengan laki-laki lain yang kemudian meninggal atau menceraikannya.

Dalam ranah muamalat (transaksi keuangan), Zad al-Mustaqni' membahas jual beli (bay'), sewa (ijarah), perwakilan (wakalah), persekutuan (syirkah), hadiah (hibah), dan waris (mawarith). Prinsip dasar adalah bahwa transaksi-transaksi diperbolehkan kecuali ada dalil yang melarangnya. Semua bentuk riba dilarang secara ketat berdasarkan larangan Al-Qur'an (Al-Baqarah: 275–279) dan berbagai hadis.

Jual beli yang sah mensyaratkan: penjual dan pembeli yang memiliki kapasitas hukum, komoditas yang spesifik, harga yang spesifik, serta ijab dan qabul yang sah. Jual beli yang mengandung gharar (ketidakpastian berlebih) dilarang. Ini mencakup menjual ikan di dalam air, hewan yang belum lahir, atau tanaman sebelum menunjukkan tanda-tanda kematangan. Nabi ﷺ melarang transaksi-transaksi semacam itu untuk melindungi kedua pihak dari kerugian finansial.

Syirkah (persekutuan) dalam berbagai bentuknya diperbolehkan. Yang paling umum dalam fikih klasik adalah: syirkah al-'inan (persekutuan modal di mana setiap mitra berkontribusi modal dan keuntungan dibagi secara proporsional), syirkah al-abdan (persekutuan kerja di antara para pengrajin), dan syirkah al-wujuh (persekutuan kredit). Semua bentuk mensyaratkan kesepakatan yang jelas tentang pembagian keuntungan dan menanggung kerugian sesuai dengan kontribusi modal.

Zad al-Mustaqni' menutup pembahasannya tentang muamalat dengan menekankan bahwa transaksi-transaksi komersial seorang Muslim harus mewujudkan kejujuran, transparansi, dan keadilan — kualitas-kualitas yang Nabi ﷺ contohkan sebagai pedagang di masa mudanya dan puji-puji pada orang lain sepanjang hidupnya.
content_ar: The الباب on nikah (marriage) in Zad al-Mustaqni' begins with the legal standing of marriage: يجب for one who fears falling into fornication and is able to fulfill marital duties; recommended for one who has desire and ability; and permissible for one who has no desire but no الخوف of harm. This nuanced approach reflects the الحنبلي attention to individual circumstances in determining legal rulings. The pillars of a valid marriage contract in الحنبلي الفقه are three: the offer (ijab) from the guardian (wali) or his representative, the acceptance (qabul) from the groom or his representative, and the presence of two witnesses. The الحنبلي school, like the المالكي and الشافعي schools, requires the wali for the validity of the marriage contract. النبي ﷺ (ﷺ) said: 'There is no marriage without a guardian' (أحمد, أبو داود, at-الترمذي — حسن صحيح). Mahrs (dowers) are obligatory upon the الزوج. No minimum amount is stipulated, though it should be something of value. إذا no mahr is specified, the الزوجة is entitled to the mahr al-mithl (the dower customary for women of her الأسرة and social standing). إذا the الزوج divorces قبل consummation and without specifying a mahr, she receives a mut'ah (الهبة of consolation) based on his means. Divorce (talaq) is permissible but strongly discouraged without legitimate العقل. النبي ﷺ (ﷺ) said: 'Of all permissible things, divorce is the most hated by الله' (أبو داود, ابن ماجه). In the الحنبلي school, divorce pronounced three times in one sitting counts as three divorces — a position that differs from some contemporary العلمي views. An irrevocably divorced woman (thrice-divorced) may not remarry her former الزوج حتى she has genuinely married another man who then dies or divorces her. In the realm of financial transactions (mu'amalat), Zad al-Mustaqni' covers sale (bay'), lease (ijarah), agency (wakalah), partnership (sharikah), gifts (hibah), and inheritance (mawarith). The foundational principle is that transactions are permissible إلا إذا evidence prohibits them. All forms of riba (interest/usury) are strictly prohibited based on the القرآني prohibition (2:275–279) and numerous الأحاديث. A valid sale requires: a seller and buyer with legal capacity, a specific commodity, a specific price, and a valid offer and acceptance. Sales involving gharar (excessive uncertainty) are prohibited. This includes selling fish in water, unborn animals, or crops قبل they show signs of maturity. النبي ﷺ (ﷺ) forbade such transactions to protect both parties from financial harm. Partnership (sharikah) in its various forms is permitted. The most common in الكلاسيكي الفقه are: sharikah al-'inan (capital partnership where each partner contributes capital and profits are shared proportionally), sharikah al-abdan (labor partnership among craftsmen), and sharikah al-wujuh (credit partnership).
